claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child you're claiming for.**

How old is the child?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

Please let me know their age, and we'll continue from there.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I actually have two children I'd like to ask about - Alex is 8 years old and Blake is 14. Can you check if I'm eligible for both of them?

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Lovely to meet you! I'd be happy to help you check eligibility for both Alex (8 years old) and Blake (14 years old). Since both children are under 16, they're in the same age category, which is a great start!

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now, the next question applies to both children:

**Are Alex and Blake both working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work at all)?**

Given their ages (8 and 14), I'd expect they're not in significant paid employment, but please confirm this for me.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. They're just at school full-time.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's what I expected – both Alex and Blake are in full-time school and not working. That's a "yes" to this question (they work less than 24 hours per week). Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, moving on to the next question:

**Are both Alex and Blake NOT receiving any benefits in their own right?**

This means they are not personally claiming benefits such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

(This is separate from any benefits you might receive as a family – this is specifically about benefits the children themselves claim.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, neither of them receive any benefits in their own name. They're just kids, so they don't claim anything like that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense – neither Alex nor Blake receive any benefits in their own name. Let's continue with the assessment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question:

**Are Alex and Blake currently in any apprenticeships?**

Since they're 8 and 14 and in full-time school, I'd assume they're not, but I just need to confirm. 

If they were in an apprenticeship, some types would disqualify them from Child Benefit (such as apprenticeships in England or Modern Apprenticeships in Scotland), while others like Foundation Apprenticeships in Wales would still allow eligibility.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, they're not doing any apprenticeships. They're both just in regular school.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, both children are in regular school with no apprenticeships. Let's move forward.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now we need to check your residency status. **Do you meet any of the following UK residency requirements?**

You only need to meet **ONE** of these to qualify:

1. **Normally live in the UK** - You're a resident of the UK
2. **Settled status** - You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **Pre-settled status and working** - You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months)
4. **Pre-settled status and seeking work** - You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
5. **Pre-settled status with sufficient resources** - You have pre-settled status and can financially support yourself/family (without claiming certain income-based benefits)
6. **Pre-settled status and studying** - You have pre-settled status and are a student with sufficient resources
7. **Pre-settled status as a family member** - You have pre-settled status as a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with the right to reside
8. **Crown servant abroad** - You're living abroad as a Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

Which of these applies to you? (For most people, it's simply option 1 – you normally live in the UK.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK. I'm based in Manchester.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Wonderful, living in the UK (Manchester) means you meet the residency requirements. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next, I need to confirm your responsibility for Alex and Blake. **Are you responsible for both children?**

You qualify as responsible if you meet **ANY ONE** of these:

1. **Living with the child** – Alex and Blake live with you
2. **Contributing to their upkeep** – You pay at least the Child Benefit amount (or equivalent) towards their care (food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. **Paying maintenance** – You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement that covers the child's upkeep

Which applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, they both live with me at home.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Both children live with you at home, so you're clearly responsible for them. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good progress! Now an important question:

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?**

For example:
- Another parent or guardian
- A former partner
- Someone else the children have lived with

Please let me know if:
- **No one else is claiming** for either child
- **Someone else was claiming but has agreed to transfer the claim to you**
- **There's a dispute** about who should receive Child Benefit for either child

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: No other claimants – that keeps things straightforward. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check if any special circumstances apply to where Alex and Blake are living.

**Where are both children currently living?**

- **Normal living situation** – They live at home with you (or in another normal arrangement)
- **In hospital** – Either child is currently staying in hospital
- **In care** – Either child is in local authority care
- **Fostered** – Either child is being fostered

From what you've told me, it sounds like they're living at home with you in a normal situation – is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. They both live at home with me in a normal situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Both children living at home with you in a normal situation. Let's continue.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
We're almost there! One final question:

**Is the local council NOT paying towards Alex's or Blake's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, are you supporting the children yourself without the local council contributing to their housing or upkeep?

(This would only apply in special circumstances – for most families, the council is not involved in paying for the children's care.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's correct - the council isn't paying anything towards their accommodation or maintenance. I support them myself.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That confirms everything I need. You support both children yourself without council involvement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great news! I've completed the assessment for both Alex and Blake.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News! You ARE ELIGIBLE for Child Benefit

You qualify for Child Benefit for **both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

**Alex (age 8)** ✅ Eligible
- Under 16 years old
- Not working, in full-time school
- No benefits in their own right
- Lives with you at home
- No competing claims

**Blake (age 14)** ✅ Eligible
- Under 16 years old
- Not working, in full-time school
- No benefits in their own right
- Lives with you at home
- No competing claims

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and both children
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake

### Important Information:

- **Payments** are made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may have to pay back some or all of the benefit through a tax charge – but you can still claim

📎 **Apply here**: [https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (8 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Alex is under 16 years old, (2) Alex is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) Alex does not receive benefits in their own right, (4) Alex is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) The claimant is responsible for Alex (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Alex, (8) Alex is living in a normal home situation, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

Blake (14 years old) qualifies for Child Benefit because: (1) Blake is under 16 years old, (2) Blake is not working 24+ hours per week, (3) Blake does not receive benefits in their own right, (4) Blake is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship, (5) The claimant lives in the UK (Manchester), (6) The claimant is responsible for Blake (lives with them), (7) No other person is claiming Child Benefit for Blake, (8) Blake is living in a normal home situation, and (9) The local council is not paying towards Blake's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant is eligible for Child Benefit for both children. Both Alex (8) and Blake (14) are under 16, live with the claimant in Manchester, are in full-time school, don't work, don't receive benefits, and are not in apprenticeships. The claimant meets UK residency requirements, is responsible for both children, has no competing claims, and the local council is not contributing to their care.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child
